info.text: CSIR NET level MCQ questions on Complex Integration: Cauchy's theorem, Cauchy integral formula, residues, contour integrals, evaluation of real integrals via residues, Rouché's theorem, winding numbers.

questiontext.text: Cauchy's integral formula: if \(f\) is analytic in a simply connected domain containing the closed disk \(|z-z_0|\leq r\), then \(f(z_0) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_{|z-z_0|=r}\dfrac{f(z)}{z-z_0}\,dz\). Evaluate \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=2}\dfrac{e^z}{z-1}\,dz\):

feedback.text: \(f(z)=e^z\) is analytic everywhere, and \(z_0=1\) is inside \(|z|=2\). By the Cauchy integral formula: \(\oint_{|z|=2}\frac{e^z}{z-1}dz = 2\pi i \cdot e^1 = 2\pi i e\).
Zero would result if the integrand were analytic inside the contour. But there is a pole at \(z=1\) inside \(|z|=2\), giving a non-zero result by Cauchy's theorem.
The Cauchy formula gives \(2\pi i f(z_0) = 2\pi i e\), not \(\pi i e\).
The singularity is at \(z_0=1\), so \(f(z_0)=e^1=e\), not \(e^2\).

name.text: CA26-Q02 Cauchy Integral Formula for Derivatives

questiontext.text: The higher-order Cauchy formula: \(f^{(n)}(z_0) = \dfrac{n!}{2\pi i}\oint_\gamma\dfrac{f(z)}{(z-z_0)^{n+1}}\,dz\). Evaluate \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=1}\dfrac{\cos z}{z^3}\,dz\):

feedback.text: With \(f(z)=\cos z\) and \(z_0=0\), \(n=2\): \(\oint\frac{\cos z}{z^3}dz = \frac{2\pi i}{2!}f''(0)\). Now \(f(z)=\cos z\), \(f''(z)=-\cos z\), so \(f''(0)=-1\). Wait: \(\oint\frac{\cos z}{z^3}dz = \frac{2\pi i}{2!}(-1) = -\pi i\)? Recheck: \(f''(0) = -\cos 0 = -1\). Result: \(\frac{2\pi i}{2}(-1) = -\pi i\). The correct answer is \(-\pi i\).
By the Cauchy derivative formula with \(n=2\), \(f(z)=\cos z\): \(\oint\frac{\cos z}{z^3}dz = \frac{2\pi i}{2!}f''(0) = \pi i\cdot(-\cos 0) = \pi i\cdot(-1) = -\pi i\). This is correct.
This would be the result of \(\oint\frac{\cos z}{z}dz = 2\pi i\cos(0)=2\pi i\), not for \(z^3\) in the denominator.
\(f''(0) = -\cos(0) = -1\), so the integral is \(\pi i \times (-1) = -\pi i\), not \(+\pi i\).

name.text: CA26-Q03 Residue at a Simple Pole

questiontext.text: The residue of \(f\) at a simple pole \(z_0\) is \(\text{Res}(f;z_0) = \lim_{z\to z_0}(z-z_0)f(z)\). For a simple pole of \(p(z)/q(z)\) where \(q(z_0)=0, q'(z_0)\neq0, p(z_0)\neq0\): \(\text{Res}(f;z_0) = p(z_0)/q'(z_0)\). Compute \(\text{Res}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{z^2+1};\, i\right)\):

feedback.text: The residue is complex: \(1/(2i) = -i/2\). Do not confuse with the modulus \(|1/(2i)|=1/2\).
The residue is \(-i/2\), which is purely imaginary. \(-1/2\) is real and incorrect.

questiontext.text: The Residue Theorem: if \(f\) is analytic inside and on a simple closed positively-oriented curve \(\gamma\) except at finitely many poles \(z_1,\ldots,z_n\) inside \(\gamma\), then \(\oint_\gamma f(z)\,dz = 2\pi i\sum_{k=1}^n\text{Res}(f;z_k)\). Evaluate \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=3}\dfrac{z^2}{(z-1)(z-2)}\,dz\):

feedback.text: Poles at \(z=1,2\) (both inside \(|z|=3\)). \(\text{Res}(f;1) = \lim_{z\to1}(z-1)\frac{z^2}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{1}{1-2} = -1\). \(\text{Res}(f;2) = \frac{4}{2-1} = 4\). Sum: \(-1+4=3\). Integral: \(2\pi i\cdot3 = 6\pi i\). Correction: residue at 1 is \(1^2/(1-2)=-1\) and at 2 is \(4/(2-1)=4\), total \(= 3\). Answer: \(6\pi i\).
Res at \(z=1\): \(\frac{1^2}{1-2}=-1\). Res at \(z=2\): \(\frac{4}{2-1}=4\). Sum = 3. Integral = \(2\pi i\times3=6\pi i\). This is correct.
Residue sum is \(-1+4=3\), so integral is \(6\pi i\), not \(4\pi i\).
Zero is the result when there are no poles inside the contour or when residues cancel. Here residue sum = 3, giving \(6\pi i\).

name.text: CA26-Q05 Real Integral via Residues — Rational Function

questiontext.text: To evaluate \(\displaystyle I = \int_{-\infty}^\infty \dfrac{dx}{1+x^4}\), we integrate \(f(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+z^4}\) over a semicircular contour in the upper half-plane. The poles of \(f\) in the upper half-plane are at:

feedback.text: Zeros of \(1+z^4\): \(z^4 = -1 = e^{i\pi}\), so \(z_k = e^{i(\pi+2k\pi)/4}\) for \(k=0,1,2,3\). The four roots are \(e^{i\pi/4}, e^{3i\pi/4}, e^{5i\pi/4}, e^{7i\pi/4}\). The first two lie in the upper half-plane (\(\text{Im}>0\)).
These satisfy \(z^2=-1\), not \(z^4=-1\). The poles of \(1/(1+z^4)\) are the fourth roots of \(-1\).
\(z=\pm i\) are the zeros of \(z^2+1\), not of \(z^4+1\). The fourth roots of \(-1\) are \(e^{i\pi(2k+1)/4}\).

answer.text: \(z_1 = e^{i\pi/4}\) only (one pole in upper half-plane)

feedback.text: Both \(e^{i\pi/4}\) and \(e^{3i\pi/4}\) have positive imaginary part, so both lie in the upper half-plane.

questiontext.text: Jordan's lemma: if \(f(z)\to 0\) uniformly as \(|z|\to\infty\) on the upper semicircle, then for \(a>0\): \(\int_{C_R} f(z)e^{iaz}\,dz \to 0\) as \(R\to\infty\), where \(C_R\) is the upper semicircle of radius \(R\). This is used to evaluate:

feedback.text: Consider \(f(z)=e^{iz}/z\) integrated over the keyhole contour (indented semicircle). The imaginary part of the contour integral gives \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty \frac{\sin x}{x}dx = \pi\), so \(\int_0^\infty \frac{\sin x}{x}dx = \pi/2\). Jordan's lemma ensures the semicircular arc contribution vanishes.
The Gaussian integral is evaluated by squaring and converting to polar coordinates, not by the residue theorem or Jordan's lemma.
This is \(\Gamma(1/2)=\sqrt{\pi}\), evaluated by the substitution \(x=t^2\), not by contour integration.
This is a standard real integral that needs no contour methods. Jordan's lemma is used for improper integrals over the real line involving oscillatory factors.

questiontext.text: Rouché's theorem: if \(f\) and \(g\) are analytic inside and on a simple closed curve \(\gamma\), and \(|g(z)| < |f(z)|\) for all \(z\in\gamma\), then \(f\) and \(f+g\) have the same number of zeros inside \(\gamma\). How many zeros does \(p(z) = z^5 + 3z + 1\) have inside \(|z|=2\)?

feedback.text: On \(|z|=2\): \(|z^5|=32\) and \(|3z+1|\leq3\times2+1=7 < 32\). By Rouché with \(f=z^5\) and \(g=3z+1\): \(p(z)=f+g\) has same number of zeros as \(f=z^5\) inside \(|z|=2\), which is 5 (the origin with multiplicity 5).
On \(|z|=2\), the dominant term is \(z^5\), which has 5 zeros inside. Rouché with this dominant term gives 5 zeros for \(p\).
By the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, a degree-5 polynomial has 5 zeros in \(\mathbb{C}\). Since \(p\) has 5 zeros total and we can verify all are inside \(|z|=2\) by Rouché, the answer is 5.
Rouché's theorem with the dominant term \(z^5\) on \(|z|=2\) gives exactly 5 zeros inside the disk, not 4.

questiontext.text: The winding number of a closed curve \(\gamma\) around a point \(a\notin\gamma\) is \(n(\gamma,a) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_\gamma\dfrac{dz}{z-a}\). For the curve \(\gamma(t)=2e^{2\pi it}\) (\(t\in[0,1]\), a circle traversed once counterclockwise), what is \(n(\gamma, 0)\) and \(n(\gamma, 3)\)?

feedback.text: \(\gamma\) is the circle \(|z|=2\) traversed counterclockwise once. The origin \(a=0\) is inside: \(n(\gamma,0)=1\). The point \(a=3\) is outside \(|z|=2\): \(n(\gamma,3)=0\). The winding number counts the number of times the curve winds around the point (positive = counterclockwise).
The curve is traversed once (\(t\) goes from 0 to 1), so the winding number around any interior point is 1, not 2.
\(a=3\) is outside the circle \(|z|=2\), so the winding number is 0, not 1.
This has the values interchanged. The interior point has winding number 1, and the exterior point has winding number 0.

questiontext.text: To evaluate \(I = \displaystyle\int_0^{2\pi}\dfrac{d\theta}{2+\cos\theta}\), substitute \(z=e^{i\theta}\), \(\cos\theta = (z+z^{-1})/2\), \(d\theta = dz/(iz)\). Then \(I = \oint_{|z|=1}\dfrac{dz/iz}{2+(z+z^{-1})/2}\). The result is:

feedback.text: After substitution: \(I = \oint_{|z|=1}\frac{2\,dz}{i(z^2+4z+1)}\). Roots of \(z^2+4z+1=0\): \(z = -2\pm\sqrt{3}\). Only \(z_0=-2+\sqrt{3}\approx-0.27\) is inside \(|z|=1\). \(\text{Res} = \frac{2}{i(2z_0+4)} = \frac{2}{i\cdot2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{1}{i\sqrt{3}}\). By residue theorem: \(I = 2\pi i \cdot\frac{1}{i\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{3}}\).
Off by a factor of 2. The full integral over \([0,2\pi]\) gives \(2\pi/\sqrt{3}\), not \(\pi/\sqrt{3}\).
For \(\int_0^{2\pi}d\theta/(a+b\cos\theta)\), the answer involves \(2\pi/\sqrt{a^2-b^2}\). Here \(a=2, b=1\): \(2\pi/\sqrt{3}\).
The formula gives \(2\pi/\sqrt{a^2-b^2} = 2\pi/\sqrt{4-1} = 2\pi/\sqrt{3}\), not \(2\pi/3\).

questiontext.text: To evaluate \(I = \displaystyle\int_0^\infty \dfrac{x^{p-1}}{1+x}\,dx\) for \(0<p<1\), use a keyhole contour with branch cut along \([0,\infty)\) and the principal branch of \(z^{p-1}\). The result is:

feedback.text: The keyhole contour gives: on upper branch, the integral is \(I\); on lower branch (with phase \(e^{2\pi i(p-1)}\)) it is \(-e^{2\pi ip}I\). The pole of \(z^{p-1}/(1+z)\) at \(z=-1=e^{i\pi}\) gives residue \(e^{i\pi(p-1)}\). So \(I(1-e^{2\pi ip}) = 2\pi i e^{i\pi p}\). Thus \(I = \frac{2\pi i e^{i\pi p}}{1-e^{2\pi ip}} = \frac{\pi}{\sin(\pi p)}\).
The cotangent appears in the partial fraction expansion of \(\pi\cot(\pi z)\), not in this integral. The result is \(\pi/\sin(\pi p)\).
The result is \(\pi/\sin(\pi p)\), not \(\pi/\cos(\pi p)\). These differ; for instance at \(p=1/2\): \(\pi/\sin(\pi/2)=\pi\), while \(\pi/\cos(\pi/2)\) is undefined.
By the reflection formula \(\Gamma(p)\Gamma(1-p) = \pi/\sin(\pi p)\). So this is actually equal to the correct answer, but it's not in the simplest closed form.

name.text: CA26-Q11 Meromorphic Functions — Residue at Higher-Order Pole

questiontext.text: For a pole of order \(m\) at \(z_0\): \(\text{Res}(f;z_0) = \dfrac{1}{(m-1)!}\lim_{z\to z_0}\dfrac{d^{m-1}}{dz^{m-1}}\left[(z-z_0)^m f(z)\right]\). Compute \(\text{Res}\!\left(\dfrac{e^z}{(z-1)^3};\,1\right)\):

feedback.text: Pole of order 3 at \(z_0=1\). \(\text{Res} = \frac{1}{2!}\lim_{z\to1}\frac{d^2}{dz^2}[e^z] = \frac{1}{2}e^1 = e/2\). Alternatively, expand \(e^z = e\cdot e^{z-1} = e\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(z-1)^n}{n!}\), so the coefficient of \((z-1)^2\) in \(e^z\) is \(e/2\), which is the residue.
The residue formula uses the \((m-1)!\) in the denominator. For \(m=3\): divide by \(2! = 2\), giving \(e/2\), not \(e\).
This would be the coefficient of \((z-1)^3\) in the expansion of \(e^z\). The residue is the coefficient of \((z-1)^2\), which is \(e/2!\cdot1/1 = e/2\).
The evaluation is at \(z=1\) (where \(e^z = e\)), not at \(z=3\). The answer is \(e/2\).

name.text: CA26-Q12 Cauchy's Theorem — Deformation of Contour

questiontext.text: By Cauchy's theorem, if two simple closed curves \(\gamma_1\) and \(\gamma_2\) can be continuously deformed into each other without crossing a singularity of \(f\), then \(\oint_{\gamma_1}f\,dz = \oint_{\gamma_2}f\,dz\). Evaluate \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z-i|=3}\dfrac{dz}{z^2+1}\) (note: \(z^2+1=(z-i)(z+i)\)):

feedback.text: The poles are at \(z=\pm i\). Check which are inside \(|z-i|=3\): \(|i-i|=0<3\) (inside) and \(|-i-i|=2<3\) (also inside). \(\text{Res}(f;i)=1/(2i)=-i/2\) and \(\text{Res}(f;-i)=1/(-2i)=i/2\). Sum: \(-i/2+i/2=0\). Integral: \(2\pi i\times0=0\). Actually recheck: \(\text{Res}(f;i)=\lim_{z\to i}(z-i)/(z+i)^{...}\) Wait: \(f = 1/[(z-i)(z+i)]\). Res at \(i\): \(1/(i+i)=1/(2i)\). Res at \(-i\): \(1/(-i-i)=1/(-2i)\). Sum = \(1/(2i)-1/(2i)=0\). So integral = 0. But the answer option says \(\pi\)... Let me recheck: \(1/(2i) + 1/(-2i) = 1/(2i) - 1/(2i) = 0\). So the answer should be 0.

Both poles \(z=\pm i\) lie inside \(|z-i|=3\). Residues: \(\text{Res}(f;i)=1/(2i)\) and \(\text{Res}(f;-i)=1/(-2i)\). Their sum is \(1/(2i)-1/(2i)=0\). By the residue theorem, \(\oint f\,dz = 2\pi i\times 0 = 0\). This is the correct answer.
If only the pole at \(i\) were inside, the integral would be \(2\pi i\times(-i/2)=\pi\). But both poles are inside \(|z-i|=3\), and residues cancel, giving 0.
The residues at both poles sum to zero, so the integral is 0, not \(-\pi\).

questiontext.text: Casorati-Weierstrass theorem: if \(f\) has an essential singularity at \(z_0\), then for any \(w\in\mathbb{C}\) and any \(\epsilon, \delta > 0\), there exists \(z\) with \(|z-z_0|<\delta\) and \(|f(z)-w|<\epsilon\). The stronger Picard's Great theorem says: in any punctured neighborhood of an essential singularity, \(f\) takes every complex value, with at most one exception. For \(f(z)=e^{1/z}\), the exceptional value (if any) at the essential singularity \(z=0\) is:

feedback.text:
\(e^{1/z} = 0\) would require \(1/z = -\infty\), impossible. So \(e^{1/z} 
eq 0\) for any \(z
eq0\). By Picard's theorem, 0 is the one exceptional value. Every other complex number \(w
eq0\) is taken infinitely often in any punctured neighborhood of 0.
\(e^{1/z}=1\) when \(1/z=2\pi i n\), i.e., \(z=1/(2\pi in)\), which are points near 0. So 1 IS taken (infinitely often). The exceptional value is 0.

answer.text: There is no exceptional value (all values are taken)

feedback.text: By Picard's theorem, at an essential singularity there is at most one exceptional value. For \(e^{1/z}\), the exceptional value is 0 (since \(e^w\neq 0\) for all \(w\)).
\(\infty\) is not a value in \(\mathbb{C}\). Picard's theorem concerns finite values. The exceptional value for \(e^{1/z}\) is 0.

questiontext.text: The Mean Value Property (Gauss): if \(f\) is analytic in a disk \(|z-z_0|<R\), then \(f(z_0) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}f(z_0+re^{i\theta})\,d\theta\) for any \(0<r<R\). This shows analytic functions satisfy which property shared with harmonic functions?

answer.text: The value at the center of any disk equals the average of the values on the boundary circle

feedback.text: This is the complex version of the harmonic mean value property. Since \(\text{Re}(f)\) and \(\text{Im}(f)\) are harmonic, both satisfy the MVP. The complex MVP follows directly from Cauchy's integral formula: \(f(z_0) = \frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_{|z-z_0|=r}\frac{f(z)}{z-z_0}dz = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}f(z_0+re^{i\theta})d\theta\).

answer.text: The function is constant on every circle centered at \(z_0\)

feedback.text: Non-constant analytic functions are not constant on circles. The MVP says the CENTER value equals the AVERAGE on the circle, not that the function is constant on the circle.

answer.text: The function has zero integral over every closed contour

feedback.text: Zero integral over closed contours is Cauchy's theorem (for analytic functions with no singularities). The MVP is a different (though related) statement about the center value.

answer.text: The function is smooth (infinitely differentiable) at every point

feedback.text: Smoothness follows from the Cauchy integral formula for derivatives, not from the mean value property directly.

questiontext.text: Evaluate \(\displaystyle I = \int_0^\infty \dfrac{x^2}{1+x^4}\,dx\) by contour integration. Using the upper semicircular contour and the poles of \(f(z)=z^2/(1+z^4)\) in the upper half-plane at \(z_{1,2}=e^{i\pi/4}, e^{3i\pi/4}\), the result is:

feedback.text: \(\text{Res}(f;e^{i\pi/4}) = \frac{e^{i\pi/2}}{4e^{3i\pi/4}} = \frac{e^{-i\pi/4}}{4}\). \(\text{Res}(f;e^{3i\pi/4}) = \frac{e^{3i\pi/2}}{4e^{9i\pi/4}} = \frac{e^{-3i\pi/4}\cdot(-i)}{4}\). Sum of residues: after calculation, Im-part gives total real integral \(I = \pi/(2\sqrt{2})\). One can verify: \(\int_0^\infty x^2/(1+x^4)dx = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}\).
Incorrect. The residue calculation gives \(\pi/(2\sqrt{2})\). \(\pi/4\approx0.785\) while \(\pi/(2\sqrt{2})\approx1.11\).
\(\pi\sqrt{2}/4 = \pi/(2\sqrt{2})\), which equals the correct answer. These are the same value.
The value \(\pi/2\) corresponds to \(\int_0^\infty dx/(1+x^2)\). For \(x^2/(1+x^4)\), the answer is \(\pi/(2\sqrt{2})\).

name.text: CA26-Q16 Infinite Product for Gamma Function

questiontext.text: The Gamma function satisfies \(\Gamma(z+1)=z\Gamma(z)\) and the reflection formula \(\Gamma(z)\Gamma(1-z) = \dfrac{\pi}{\sin(\pi z)}\). Using this, evaluate \(\Gamma(1/2)\):

feedback.text: Set \(z=1/2\): \(\Gamma(1/2)\Gamma(1/2) = \pi/\sin(\pi/2) = \pi/1 = \pi\). So \([\Gamma(1/2)]^2 = \pi\), giving \(\Gamma(1/2) = \sqrt{\pi}\) (since \(\Gamma\) is positive on \((0,\infty)\)). This can also be verified directly: \(\Gamma(1/2) = \int_0^\infty t^{-1/2}e^{-t}dt = 2\int_0^\infty e^{-u^2}du = \sqrt{\pi}\).
\(\Gamma(1/2) = \sqrt{\pi}\), not \(\pi\). We have \([\Gamma(1/2)]^2 = \pi\).
\(\Gamma(1) = 1\), not \(\Gamma(1/2)\). The value \(\Gamma(1/2) = \sqrt{\pi} \approx 1.772\).
Incorrect. From the reflection formula: \([\Gamma(1/2)]^2=\pi\), so \(\Gamma(1/2)=\sqrt{\pi}\neq\pi/2\).

questiontext.text: The Schwarz Reflection Principle: if \(f\) is analytic in the upper half-disk, continuous up to the real axis, and real on the real axis, then \(f\) extends analytically to the whole disk by \(f(\bar{z}) = \overline{f(z)}\). This principle is used to show that:

answer.text: An analytic function with real values on the real axis has coefficients \(a_n\) that are all real in its Taylor series

feedback.text: If \(f\) is analytic in a disk centered on the real axis and \(f(x)\in\mathbb{R}\) for real \(x\), then by the Schwarz reflection principle \(f(\bar{z})=\overline{f(z)}\). Expanding: \(\sum a_n\bar{z}^n = \overline{\sum a_n z^n} = \sum\bar{a}_n\bar{z}^n\), so \(a_n=\bar{a}_n\), i.e., all coefficients are real.

answer.text: Every analytic function maps the real line to the real line

feedback.text: This is false in general. \(f(z)=iz\) maps real numbers to imaginary numbers. The reflection principle applies only when \(f\) is real on the real axis.

answer.text: Analytic continuation across the real axis always exists

feedback.text: Analytic continuation across the real axis is guaranteed only when the function is real on the real axis and satisfies the conditions of the reflection principle. In general, there may be singularities on the real axis.

answer.text: The imaginary part of \(f\) is always zero on the real axis

feedback.text: This is a hypothesis (condition) of the reflection principle, not a conclusion. The principle uses this to extend the function.

questiontext.text: The Mittag-Leffler theorem guarantees the existence of a meromorphic function with prescribed poles and principal parts. The partial fraction expansion of \(\pi\cot(\pi z)\) is a classical example: \(\pi\cot(\pi z) = \dfrac{1}{z} + \sum_{n=1}^\infty\left(\dfrac{1}{z-n}+\dfrac{1}{z+n}\right)\). This converges for:

feedback.text: The series \(\frac{1}{z}+\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{2z}{z^2-n^2}\) converges absolutely and uniformly on compact subsets of \(\mathbb{C}\setminus\mathbb{Z}\). The individual groupings \(\frac{1}{z-n}+\frac{1}{z+n}=\frac{2z}{z^2-n^2}=O(n^{-2})\) ensure convergence.
The Mittag-Leffler expansion is valid for all non-integer \(z\), not just inside the unit disk.
The expansion holds throughout \(\mathbb{C}\setminus\mathbb{Z}\), including both half-planes.
The pairing \(\frac{1}{z-n}+\frac{1}{z+n} = O(1/n^2)\) ensures convergence. The individual terms \(1/(z-n)\) would diverge, but grouped pairs converge.

questiontext.text: Classify the singularity of \(f(z) = \dfrac{\sin z}{z^3}\) at \(z = 0\):

feedback.text: \(\sin z = z - z^3/6 + z^5/120 - \cdots\), so \(\frac{\sin z}{z^3} = \frac{1}{z^2} - \frac{1}{6} + \frac{z^2}{120} - \cdots\). The Laurent expansion has a leading term \(1/z^2\), so \(z=0\) is a pole of order 2, not order 1. Actually: the Laurent series is \(z^{-2} - 1/6 + \cdots\), which shows a pole of order 2.
\(\sin z/z^3 = (z - z^3/6 + \cdots)/z^3 = 1/z^2 - 1/6 + z^2/120 - \cdots\). The lowest power is \(z^{-2}\), so this is a pole of order 2. This is correct.
If it were removable, \(\lim_{z\to0}f(z)\) would be finite. But \(\lim_{z\to0}\sin(z)/z^3 = \lim_{z\to0}1/z^2 = \infty\). Not removable.
Essential singularities have infinitely many negative powers in their Laurent series. Here only finitely many (specifically, \(z^{-2}\)), so it's a pole.

questiontext.text: When integrating along the real axis and the integrand has a simple pole at a real point \(x_0\), an indented contour (small semicircle of radius \(\epsilon\) around \(x_0\)) is used. The contribution of the small semicircle at a simple pole as \(\epsilon\to0\) is:

answer.text: \(-\pi i \cdot\text{Res}(f;x_0)\) (for a lower indentation that goes below the pole)

feedback.text: For a semicircle of radius \(\epsilon\) going below the real axis (clockwise, subtending angle \(-\pi\) to \(0\)), the integral over the arc converges to \(-\pi i\cdot\text{Res}(f;x_0)\) as \(\epsilon\to0\). For an upper indentation (counterclockwise), the contribution is \(+\pi i\cdot\text{Res}(f;x_0)\). The sign depends on the orientation.
For simple poles on the contour, the small semicircle contributes \(\pm\pi i\times\text{Res}\neq0\). Only removable singularities give zero contribution.
The full \(2\pi i\times\text{Res}\) comes from a full circle. A small semicircle gives half that (\(\pm\pi i\times\text{Res}\)), with the sign depending on orientation.

answer.text: The contribution depends on \(\epsilon\) and does not converge

feedback.text: For simple poles, the contribution of the small semicircle converges to \(\pm\pi i\times\text{Res}\) as \(\epsilon\to0\). This is a standard result used in the evaluation of principal value integrals.
